Can you explain the difference between Béchamel and Velouté?

Sure! Béchamel is made with milk and a white roux, giving it a smooth, creamy texture perfect for topping casseroles. Velouté, on the other hand, uses a light stock and a blond roux, resulting in a lighter, more delicate sauce often used as a base for poultry or fish dishes.

That sounds interesting. How does the temperature affect this reaction?

Higher temperatures speed up the Maillard reaction. It starts around 305°F (151°C) but really kicks in at about 347°F (175°C). That's why roasting or searing foods at higher temperatures can intensify their flavors, making them more savory and complex.

Sure! Sautéing uses high heat with a small amount of fat to quickly cook smaller pieces like vegetables or thin cuts of meat. Roasting, on the other hand, involves dry heat in an oven for even browning, perfect for larger pieces of meat or whole vegetables.
That makes sense. Can you give me a tip for achieving the best results when sautéing?
Absolutely! Make sure your pan is hot and use enough oil to coat the bottom lightly. This will help prevent sticking and ensure even cooking with that lovely golden-brown texture.
